---
title: "Feature Tour"
subtitle: "A guided route through inputs, feature families, outputs, and downstream workflows."
sidebar: tour
---

This tour is the first pass through `natural_features`: what you can give it, what you can ask for, and what the library returns.

## One route through the library

::: {.tour-route}
::: {.tour-step}
#### 1. Bring in a stimulus
Use text strings, paths, arrays, or typed stimulus wrappers such as `TextStimulus`, `AudioStimulus`, `ImageStimulus`, `VideoStimulus`, and `MultiModalStimulus`.
:::

::: {.tour-step}
#### 2. Inspect the catalogue
`available_features()` reports feature IDs, modalities, output schemas, dependency classes, tags, bundles, and opt-in status.
:::

::: {.tour-step}
#### 3. Plan or extract
`plan_features()` resolves inputs and parameters without running extractors. `extract_features()` executes the plan and returns typed feature objects.
:::

::: {.tour-step}
#### 4. Export or query
Convert outputs to tables, merge feature tables, run recipe DAGs, batch audio files, or query run-aware windows for fMRI models.
:::
:::

## What you can bring in

::: {.tour-grid}
::: {.tour-card}
#### Text and transcripts
Plain text can become word events. Existing `EventSeries` objects can feed alignment, language, phonology, and table export workflows.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Audio
WAV files and `AudioStimulus` objects support acoustic summaries, speech routes, voice activity, phonology, and batch extraction.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Images and video
Images and videos can be routed to OCR, low-level visual descriptors, face signals, neural embeddings, frame sampling, and audio extraction.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Feature objects
`FeatureSeries`, `EventSeries`, and `TrackSeries` can be validated, converted to tables, merged, resampled, lagged, convolved, and queried by time.
:::
:::

## What you can ask for today

The grid below is generated from the installed Python package when the docs render. It uses `budget="allow_python"` so optional Python-backed routes are visible, while placeholder specs remain hidden.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from collections import defaultdict
from natural_features.workflows import available_features

family_notes = {
    "affect": "Audio and visual proxy features for social or affective structure.",
    "audio": "RMS, mel, MFCC, spectral summaries, resampling, trimming, and optional openSMILE LLDs.",
    "events": "Event alignment utilities.",
    "features": "Resampling, HRF convolution, and lag expansion for FeatureSeries objects.",
    "image": "OCR routes for still images.",
    "language": "Surface controls, embedding hooks, BERT-style word embeddings, and surprisal routes.",
    "meta": "Scene boundary summaries.",
    "speech": "VAD, ASR word events, alignment, phonology, articulatory features, posteriorgrams, and SSL embeddings.",
    "text": "Word event tokenization.",
    "video": "Frame sampling, trimming, OCR, and audio extraction.",
    "vision": "Frame energy, motion, face signals, CLIP/DINO embeddings, and motion-energy routes.",
}

groups = defaultdict(list)
for entry in available_features(budget="allow_python"):
    groups[entry.feature_id.split(".", 1)[0]].append(entry)

print("::: {.tour-family-grid}")
for family in sorted(groups):
    entries = sorted(groups[family], key=lambda item: item.feature_id)
    shown = ", ".join(f"`{entry.feature_id}`" for entry in entries[:7])
    if len(entries) > 7:
        shown += f", +{len(entries) - 7} more"
    opt_in = sum(1 for entry in entries if entry.requires_opt_in)
    feature_word = "feature ID" if len(entries) == 1 else "feature IDs"
    opt_phrase = "1 requires opt-in" if opt_in == 1 else f"{opt_in} require opt-in"
    note = family_notes.get(family, "Registered extractor family.")
    print(f"""
::: {{.tour-family}}
#### {family}
{note}

[{len(entries)} {feature_word}; {opt_phrase}.]{{.tour-meta}}

::: {{.tour-feature-list}}
{shown}
:::
:::
""")
print(":::")
```

## First runnable path

Start with text because it needs no optional media backend. `extract_features()` returns an `ExtractFeaturesResult`; the feature you requested is a typed `EventSeries`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from natural_features.core.interchange import as_event_table
from natural_features.workflows import extract_features

result = extract_features(
    "The scene opens on a quiet room.",
    features=["text.tokenize"],
)
words = result.features["text.tokenize"]
labels = words.label.tolist()

print(f"""
::: {{.quick-result .tour-result}}
::: {{.quick-stat}}
[request]{{.quick-label}}

`text.tokenize`
:::
::: {{.quick-stat}}
[object]{{.quick-label}}

`{type(words).__name__}` - `{words.schema}`
:::
::: {{.quick-stat}}
[events]{{.quick-label}}

{len(words)} word events
:::
::: {{.quick-words}}
[labels]{{.quick-label}}

{", ".join(f"`{label}`" for label in labels)}
:::
:::
""")

table = as_event_table(words).loc[:, ["onset_s", "offset_s", "label", "schema"]]
print("| onset_s | offset_s | label | schema |")
print("|---:|---:|---|---|")
for row in table.itertuples(index=False):
    print(f"| {row.onset_s:g} | {row.offset_s:g} | `{row.label}` | `{row.schema}` |")
```

The object keeps event timing, labels, schema, and provenance metadata together. Table export is a view of the same object, not a separate representation to maintain by hand.

## Plan media before running it

For video, image, speech, and neural embedding routes, planning is often the first useful operation. It tells you what would run and which features require an opt-in budget.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from natural_features.workflows import plan_features

plan = plan_features(
    "video",
    features=["video.audio.extract", "vision.face"],
    budget="allow_python",
)

print("| feature | input | output | opt-in |")
print("|---|---|---|---|")
for row in plan.rows:
    opt_in = "yes" if row.requires_opt_in else "no"
    print(
        f"| `{row.feature_id}` | `{row.input_token}` | "
        f"`{row.output_schema}` | {opt_in} |"
    )
```

The same plan can become a recipe dictionary, which lets you inspect or store the route before execution.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
import yaml

print("```yaml")
print(yaml.safe_dump(plan.to_recipe(), sort_keys=False).strip())
print("```")
```

## What you can do with the outputs

::: {.tour-grid}
::: {.tour-card}
#### Keep temporal objects typed
Use `FeatureSeries` for sampled arrays, `EventSeries` for labeled intervals, and `TrackSeries` for tracked objects with framewise coordinates.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Move into tables
Use `as_feature_table()`, `as_event_table()`, `as_track_table()`, `as_temporal_table()`, and `merge_feature_tables()` when a modeling or QC step wants tabular data.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Compose recipes
Use recipe specs to express multi-step feature routes, preview them as DAGs, and run them through the local execution engine.
:::

::: {.tour-card}
#### Query fMRI windows
Use `RunGrid`, `ExperimentGrid`, and window queries to collect features by run, TR, and time window without losing stimulus provenance.
:::
:::

## Where to go next

::: {.tour-next-grid}
::: {.tour-next}
#### Build a first object
[Start with the five-page path](../start/index.qmd): wrap a stimulus, list features, extract, export, and handle optional media backends.
:::

::: {.tour-next}
#### Read the contracts
[Concepts](../concepts/index.qmd) explains feature objects, planning, execution modes, recipe DAGs, table interchange, and fMRI windows.
:::

::: {.tour-next}
#### Copy a task recipe
[Cookbook](../cookbook/index.qmd) contains runnable examples for text tokenization, media planning, audio batches, table export, and fMRI window queries.
:::
:::
